source_metadata.title_raw: Gregory G. Diaz - A-19-021 -  March 22, 2019 - Ventura

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Gregory G. Diaz 
City Attorney 
City of Ventura 
501 Poli Street, Room 213 
Ventura, CA 93001 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-021 
Dear Mr. Diaz: 
March 22, 2019 
. This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
May San Buenaventura City Councilmember Jim Friedman take part in governmental 
decisions related to a potential "Olivas Park Specific Plan," given that he previously received 
income from an Olivas Park property owner the City would likely negotiate with? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. Because the property owner has not been a source of Councilmember Friedman's 
income during the previous 12 months, and the facts do not implicate other potential economic 
interests, nothing in the Act prohibits Councilmember Friedman from taking part in governmental 
decisions related to the Olivas Park Specific Plan. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR 
File No. A-19-021 
Page No. 2 
Jim Friedman was elected to the City Council of San Buenaventura in November 2018 and 
took office the following month. 
The City Council plans on holding a public hearing on whether to approve a new specific 
plan for the Olivas Park area of the City. The property in the territory covered by the proposed 
Olivas Park Specific Plan is currently largely undevelopable because it is in a floodplain. One of the 
essential elements of the proposed Specific Plan is the construction of a levee that would remove 
the territory from the floodplain. In addition, approval of the Specific Plan would also move 
forward the completion of a road improvement project that will link previously unconnected 
segments of the City's roadway system. The proposed land uses include both commercial and 
potentially industrial uses. In addition to consideration of the proposed Specific Plan, the City 
Council will also need to take actions on related environmental documents, under the California 
Environmental Quality Act. If approved, a development agreement would be negotiated between 
the City and Olivas Park property owners. There may be other entitlements and actions the City 
Council would need to take if the Specific Plan is approved. 
Between March 2004 and November 2017, Councilmember Friedman provided consulting 
services to John Hofer, the owner of a significant amount of property within the proposed Olivas 
P~rk Specific Plan. As a consultant, Councilmember Friedman was present during meetings where 
issues and concepts that ultimately became part of the Olivas Park Specific Plan were discussed. 
However, Councilmember Friedman did not provide any technical advice regarding a specific plan; 
rather, he gave advice related to the "optics" of the ideas and concepts discussed at the meetings. 
Councilmember Friedman's work for Mr. Hofer was as an independent consultant, not as an 
employee or partner, and he does not have any ownership interest in Mr. Hofer's business entities. 
Councilmember Friedman has no new contracts or continuing agreements with Mr. Hofer or any of 
his business entities and does not anticipate receiving them while on the City Council. 
ANALYSIS 
Under Section 87100, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local government shall 
make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." A 
public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from 
its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on 
certain enumerated economic interests. (Section 87103.) Such economic interests include "[a]ny 
source of income ... aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or 
promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is 
made." 
Here, the only economic interest potentially implicated by the facts provided is a source-of-
income interest in Mr. Hofer. However, it has been longer than 12 months since Councilmember 
Friedman received income from Mr. Hofer. Accordingly, Councilmember Friedman does not have a 


File No. A-19-021 
Page No. 3 
disqualifying financial interest that would prohibit him from taking part in governmental decisions 
related to the Olivas Park Specific Plan. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division
